claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 4 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority; Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child in local authority care for 9 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit. To do this properly, I'll need to gather some information about you and your children.

Let me start with a basic question:

**How many children do you have that you're inquiring about for Child Benefit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm inquiring about my 1 child - Alex, who is 4 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you're inquiring about Alex, who is 4 years old.

To assess your eligibility, I need to understand your living situation better.

**Does Alex live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex does live with me, but their other parent also lives with us and they already claim Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is important information.

Before I can give you a final answer, I need to check the specific rules around this situation. Let me review the relevant guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the guidance I've reviewed, I can now provide you with an outcome.

The key rule is: **Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child.** 

Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, and you both live with Alex, you would not be eligible to also claim Child Benefit for the same child. Child Benefit can only be claimed by one person per child.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Summary**

Unfortunately, you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex. While Alex meets all the other eligibility criteria (being under 16 and living with you in the UK), only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child. Since the other parent is already claiming for Alex, you cannot also make a claim for the same child.

If circumstances change in the future and the other parent stops claiming, you would be able to make a claim at that time.

reasonings: Alex is 4 years old and lives with you. While you meet the basic requirements to be responsible for a child (living with them), only one person can receive Child Benefit for a child. Since Alex's other parent is already claiming Child Benefit for Alex, you cannot also claim for the same child. According to the rules, 'Only one person can get Child Benefit for a child.'
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex because the other parent is already claiming for this child. The eligibility rules state that only one person can claim Child Benefit per child, and since this claim is already being made by the other parent, the claimant cannot also claim for the same child.">
